Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see:
- Player at rows 28-31, cols 36-38 (N)
- White frame at rows 28-31, cols 39-43 (W) - shifted right
- Blue target at rows 29-30, cols 41-42 (b) - inside white frame
- Blue object at rows 28-31, cols 28-35 (b) - px=18, this is a large blue shape that grew!
- Center frame still at rows 32-35, cols 32-35 (c)
- Left frame still at rows 32-35, cols 24-27 (c)
The blue object expanded from px=10 to px=18, spanning cols 28-35. This seems to be filling the space where the center frame's area would be, but the center frame is still intact.
I need to rethink my approach. The player is at rows 28-31, cols 36-38. The center frame is at rows 32-35, cols 32-35. To break the center frame, I need to move into its space.
The player is currently ABOVE the center frame. DOWN is blocked. LEFT is blocked by the blue object. RIGHT is possible but moves away from the center frame.
Let me try: DOWN first to see if it's still blocked, or try a different approach. Actually, let me try going UP first to get more space, then LEFT around the blue object.
Wait - the blue object is at rows 28-31, cols 28-35. The player is at rows 28-31, cols 36-38. If I go UP, the player goes to rows 25-28. The blue object is at rows 28-31, so at rows 25-28 the player would partially overlap with the blue object at row 28. That might be blocked.
Let me try UP first to see what happens.

# Blue object at rows 28-31, cols 28-35 (blocking LEFT)
# Center frame at rows 32-35, cols 32-35 (blocking DOWN)
